1: yhwh šĕmaʿ tĕpillātî haʾăzînāh ʾel-taḥănûnāy baʾămûnātĕkā ʿănēnî bĕṣidqātĕkā — "O LORD, hear my prayer; listen to my pleas for mercy; in your faithfulness and righteousness, answer me" — the appeal grounds YHWH's response not in the psalmist's merit but in two divine attributes: ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness, reliability, from ʾāman — the root of ʾāmēn) and ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness, right action). The psalmist appeals to YHWH's character rather than to his own deserving — a structurally important opening that anticipates v2's disclaimer of personal righteousness.
2: wĕʾal-tābôʾ bĕmišpāṭ ʾet-ʿabdĕkā kî lōʾ-yiṣdaq lĕpānêkā kol-ḥāy — "do not bring your servant into judgment, for no living person can stand as righteous before you" — ʾal-tābôʾ bĕmišpāṭ (do not enter into judgment with) — if YHWH were to mount a full judicial review of the servant's life, the verdict would be fatal. lōʾ-yiṣdaq lĕpānêkā kol-ḥāy (no living person can be declared righteous before you) — one of the OT's most unambiguous statements of universal human moral inadequacy before the divine standard. This verse stands behind the NT's doctrine of justification by grace: if no one is righteous before God (cf. Rom 3:20, Gal 2:16 — Paul cites Ps 143:2 directly), then the only hope is God's own provision of righteousness. The verse is cited without modification in the Greek of Gal 2:16.
3: kî rādap ʾôyēb napšî dikkāʾ lāʾāreṣ ḥayyātî hôšîbanî bĕmahăšakkîm kĕmētê ʿôlām — "for the enemy has hunted me down and crushed my life to the ground; he has forced me to dwell in darkness like those who died long ago" — dikkāʾ lāʾāreṣ ḥayyātî (he crushed my life to the ground) — dākaʾ (to crush, smite, contrite — related to dakkāʾ, the crushed/contrite ones of Ps 51:17; Isa 57:15). mahăšakkîm (dark places, from ḥāšak, to be dark) — the places of darkness equivalent to Sheol. kĕmētê ʿôlām (like those who died long ago, the long-dead ones) — the psalmist is still alive but already experiencing the condition of the permanently dead.
4: waṭṭitʿaṭṭēp ʿālay rûḥî bĕtôkî yiššōmēm libbî — "so my spirit grows faint within me; my heart within me is filled with dismay/is appalled" — hiṭʿaṭṭēp rûḥî (my spirit faints/collapses, same verb as Ps 142:3) — the two cave/darkness psalms (142-143) share this vocabulary of spiritual collapse. yiššōmēm libbî (my heart is desolate/appalled, Hithpael of šāmam, to be desolate, devastated, appalled) — the word used for desolated land after catastrophe (Lam 1:13,16); the heart is a desolated landscape.
5: zākartî yāmîm miqqedem hāgîtî bĕkhol-paʿălĕkā bĕmaʿăśēh yādêkā ʾăśôḥēaḥ — "I call to mind the days of old; I meditate on all you have done; I reflect on the work of your hands" — zākartî yāmîm miqqedem (I remember the days of old) — the classic Deuteronomic move: when the present is overwhelming, look back to what YHWH has done in history. hāgîtî (I meditate, murmur, from hāgāh — the same verb as Ps 1:2's Torah-meditation). ʾăśôḥēaḥ (I reflect, muse, from śîaḥ — complaint/meditation, the same word as Ps 142:2) — here the musing is turned from complaint to contemplation of YHWH's past works.
6: pēraśtî yādāy ʾēlêkā napšî kĕʾereṣ ʿăyēpāh lĕkā selāh — "I reach out my hands toward you; my whole being longs for you like a parched land" — pēraśtî yādāy ʾēlêkā (I spread out my hands toward you) — the gesture of open-palmed prayer extending toward YHWH. napšî kĕʾereṣ ʿăyēpāh lĕkā (my soul is like a parched/thirsty land to/for you) — one of the Psalter's most vivid anthropological images: the whole living person (nepeš) is a dried, cracked landscape yearning for rain. The simile maps onto the agricultural reality of the Judean wilderness — soil that has split open from thirst literally gapes open seeking water. The soul's orientation toward YHWH is structural, like the cracked earth's openness to rain.
7: mahēr ʿănēnî yhwh kālĕtāh rûḥî ʾal-tastēr pānêkā mimmennî wĕnimšaltî ʿim-yōrĕdê bôr — "answer me quickly, O LORD — my spirit is failing! Do not hide your face from me, or I will be like those who sink into the pit" — kālĕtāh rûḥî (my spirit is finished/consumed, from kālāh, to be complete, finished, consumed) — the spirit running out like a candle nearly burned through. ʾal-tastēr pānêkā (do not hide your face) — the hidden face of YHWH as abandonment (Ps 27:9; 88:14; Isa 54:8); the presence of YHWH's face is the prerequisite for life. yōrĕdê bôr (those who go down to the pit) — bôr (a pit, cistern, also Sheol) — the alternative to YHWH's face is being like the dead.
8: hašmîʿēnî babboqer ḥasdĕkā kî-bĕkā bāṭāḥtî hôdîʿēnî derekh-zû ʾēlēk kî-ʾēlêkā nāśāʾtî napšî — "let me hear of your steadfast love in the morning, for in you I put my trust; show me the path I should take, for I lift my soul to you" — hašmîʿēnî babboqer (cause me to hear in the morning) — morning as the traditional time of divine salvation announcement (Ps 46:5: "God will help her at the break of dawn"; 90:14; Lam 3:23). The morning announcement of ḥesed (covenant love) answers the night of darkness (v3). derekh-zû ʾēlēk (the path by which I should go) — practical directional guidance embedded within the deep prayer for covenant love.
9: haṣṣîlēnî mēʾōyĕbay yhwh ʾēlêkā kissîtî — "rescue me from my enemies, O LORD — I run to you for shelter" — kissîtî (I have covered/hidden myself, from kāsāh, to cover) — the psalmist uses YHWH as a covering, a hiding place (Ps 32:7; 91:1-4). The phrase may also be read "to you I have fled for cover" — YHWH as the protective hiding place from enemy pursuit.
10: lammĕdēnî laʿăśôt rĕṣônĕkā kî-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhāy rûḥăkā ṭôbāh tanhênî bĕʾereṣ mîšôr — "teach me to do your will, for you are my God; may your good Spirit lead me on a level path" — rûḥăkā ṭôbāh (your good Spirit) — one of only three explicit references to the divine Spirit in the Psalter (Ps 51:11: "take not your Holy Spirit from me"; Ps 139:7: "where shall I flee from your Spirit?"; here). ṭôbāh (good, pleasant, beneficent) distinguishes this from a merely powerful or ambiguous spirit. tanhênî bĕʾereṣ mîšôr (lead me on a level/smooth path, from nāḥāh, to lead + mîšôr, level ground, plain, straightness) — the Spirit's leading counteracts the rough, trap-laden terrain of the enemy's path (vv3-4). The Spirit levels the road for the psalmist's walk.
11: lĕmaʿan-šimĕkā yhwh tĕḥayyēnî bĕṣidqātĕkā tôṣîʾ miṣṣārāh napšî — "for the sake of your name, O LORD, keep me alive; in your righteousness lead my soul out of its distress" — lĕmaʿan-šimĕkā (for the sake of your name) — the appeal to YHWH's own reputation rather than to the psalmist's merit (cf. Ps 25:11; 31:3; Ezek 36:22). YHWH's life-preserving action demonstrates who he is. bĕṣidqātĕkā (in your righteousness) — righteousness not as judgment (as it could be in v2's legal setting) but as salvific activity: the righteous God acts righteously by saving the one who cries to him.
12: ûbĕḥasdĕkā taṣmît ʾôyĕbāy wĕhʾăbadtā kol-ṣōrĕrê napšî kî ʾănî ʿabdĕkā — "and in your steadfast love silence my enemies and put an end to all who oppress me — for I am your servant" — ûbĕḥasdĕkā (in your ḥesed) — the psalm closes as it opened: both the appeal and the action are grounded in divine character attributes (ʾĕmûnāh and ṣĕdāqāh, v1; ḥesed, v12). kî ʾănî ʿabdĕkā (for I am your servant) — the final self-identification; the claim is not moral excellence but covenantal relationship: the servant has recourse to the master. Ps 143 moves from universal human inadequacy (v2) to the servant's particular claim on the master's ḥesed (v12).
